aliases: Italian Lamb with Olives and Artichoke

english: Italian Lamb with Olives and Artichoke

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Agnello alla Ligure; mapped to generic lamb dish class as closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific entry for Ligurian-style lamb preparation; A00LN (lamb meat, cooked) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for this specific Italian lamb preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Agnello alla Ligure weighs approximately 300 g, comprising a lamb loin portion, artichoke purée, pearl onions, and Riviera olives.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lamb, loin, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4" fat, cooked, roasted (NDB 17004) and component ingredients (artichoke, olives, onions)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Agnello alla Ligure as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition of USDA FoodData Central entries for roasted lamb loin (dominant component, ~55% by weight), artichoke purée (~20%), Riviera-style olives (~10%), pearl onions (~10%), and olive oil (~5%). Input per_100g values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA component-weighted estimate and found to be broadly consistent. Final values reflect the USDA-anchored composite estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients set to -1 where composite-level data is not reliably derivable from available approved sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on lamb and low-GI vegetable components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Agnello alla Ligure. GI estimated at approximately 20 (low) based on the dominant protein-fat matrix of roasted lamb loin (GI effectively negligible for pure protein/fat foods) combined with low-GI artichoke (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database) and pearl onions (GI ~10). The composite dish is carbohydrate-sparse (5 g/100 g), yielding a glycemic load per 300 g serving of approximately 3.0 g glucose equivalents, which is clinically low. Estimation basis documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 233 mg/100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 699 mg sodium (~30% of the 2300 mg daily limit).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should monitor portion size carefully, as a full 300 g serving contributes approximately 699 mg sodium; requesting reduced-salt preparation and pairing with potassium-rich vegetables (artichoke, onions already present) aligns with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5 g/100 g), low sugar (1.3 g/100 g), high protein (15 g/100 g), and estimated GI of ~20 support stable postprandial glycaemia.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments; the artichoke-derived prebiotic fibre (inulin) further attenuates glycaemic response, and the high protein content promotes satiety and reduces postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g and cholesterol 43 mg/100 g from lamb loin; a 300 g serving provides 12 g saturated fat (~60% of the 20 g daily limit for a 2000 kcal diet).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g and favour lean lamb loin trimmed of visible fat; the olive oil and olives contribute cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and polyphenols, partially offsetting the saturated fat burden, but frequency should be moderated to 1–2 times per week.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which can amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat frequency; the anti-inflammatory contributions of olive oil, rosemary, thyme, and garlic in this dish are beneficial but do not fully counterbalance the arachidonic acid load from lamb loin, so occasional consumption is preferred over regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Agnello alla Ligure (Ligurian-Style Lamb) is a refined Italian dish originating from the Liguria region of northwestern Italy, adapted here as a gluten-free restaurant preparation. The dish centres on a mint-brined lamb loin that is pan-seared or oven-roasted to develop a caramelised crust while retaining a tender, juicy interior. It is plated with a smooth, creamy artichoke purée made from globe artichoke hearts blended with olive oil and seasoning, sweet pearl onions sautéed until golden, and briny Riviera-style olives that provide a characteristic Ligurian flavour accent. The overall flavour profile is savoury and herbaceous, with the mint brine imparting subtle freshness to the lamb, the artichoke purée adding earthy creaminess, and the olives contributing umami depth. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense (15 g/100 g) and moderate in fat (11.7 g/100 g), with a low carbohydrate content (5 g/100 g) and an estimated glycemic index of approximately 20, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium is moderate at 233 mg/100 g, warranting portion awareness for hypertensive individuals. The dish is explicitly gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low glycaemic load and high protein content, but cardiovascular patients should moderate portion size given the saturated fat contribution from lamb. The artichoke provides prebiotic inulin fibre, supporting gut microbiome health and further moderating glycaemic response.
common_user_queries: Is lamb with artichoke purée suitable for diabetics?
Italian lamb dish with olives and artichoke
Is this dish suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No single approved source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN) contains a direct entry for Agnello alla Ligure as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are derived from a USDA-anchored weighted component estimate (roasted lamb loin, artichoke, olives, pearl onions, olive oil), cross-checked against the input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). The composite estimation introduces moderate uncertainty, particularly for micronutrients and fatty acid fractions. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore set to medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated by weighted composition from USDA FoodData Central component entries (roasted lamb loin ~55%, artichoke purée ~20%, olives ~10%, pearl onions ~10%, olive oil ~5%); no single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific composite Ligurian lamb preparation are not available as a composite in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI estimated at ~20 (low) using analogous-food method from University of Sydney GI Database entries for artichoke and onion components; lamb loin contributes negligible GI as a protein-fat dominant food. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (20 × 5.0 × 3) / 100 = 3.0 g. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not constitute a nutrient data source for this dish; it was not used as primary nutrient evidence. 5. Ethnic tags in the input ('Pasta', 'Pizza', 'Chicken Dish') were removed as they are incorrect for this lamb dish. 6. This dish is served in Singapore as a restaurant item (GrabFood listing) but is Italian in cuisine origin; multilingual Singaporean language requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply as the dish is not a Singaporean dish. 7. No core macro is zero; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered.
